Regents' Plan Will Hurt Advanced Students
Up From Affirmative Action
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The debate over affirmative action gained momentum last week when the United  States Supreme Court refused to block a Mississippi mandate that all of the state's universities, including its three traditionally black institutions, must have the same admission standards. Coming on the heels of a Federal appeals court ruling against race-based preferences at the University of Texas Law School, the decision has led some to fear that blacks will be shut out of higher education. 

     The critics are right only insofar as they say our nation has failed to build a color-blind society. The problem is that rather than equalizing playing fields, we have created government laws and programs that promote state-sponsored discrimination in the form of affirmative action. We try to convince ourselves that targeted discrimination is appropriate. It is not.

DANCE REVIEW;Visions of the Alumni, Brought to Life by Their Successors
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The dance division of the State University College here has much to celebrate. Last May, the 20th class of dance majors graduated from the college.  This fall, the entire college will celebrate its 25th anniversary. 

     The soundness of the dance training offered there was demonstrated this afternoon when the Purchase Dance Corps, a student group, appeared at the Performing Arts Center in works by five Purchase alumni who have gone on to have successful choreographic careers. Four pieces were premieres; the fifth was a work from a professional company's repertory presented for the first time by a college troupe. The dancers looked at home in them all.

nytimes_articles.headline: The Pro-Immigration Lobby
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The forces in favor of reducing legal as well as illegal immigration roared into Washington like a speeding train last year. The momentum appeared to be unstoppable. Polls showed that Americans overwhelmingly favored reductions in the number of legal migrants. Support came from a bipartisan reform commission led by the late Barbara Jordan, from President Clinton and from Republican and Democratic lawmakers, who introduced bills to change the current policy, which brought in 720,000 legal immigrants last year. 

     But in the last month, both houses of Congress rejected or sidetracked these  reductions. In the end, almost nobody was for them -- except the American people.
